ALTERNATIVE 5: DEFERMENT - Request payment deferment instead of subordination: Similar to HAP but can be structured differently, Can defer principal only or principal + interest, Typically 6-12 months, May be available multiple times over loan life. PROS: Immediate payment relief, No new debt, Preserves collateral structure. CONS: Interest continues accruing, Temporary solution only, Deferred amounts due at maturity. BEST FOR: Temporary business disruptions, Seasonal cash flow gaps, Bridge to recovery or sale. ALTERNATIVE 6: LOAN MODIFICATION - Request modification of COVID EIDL terms: Extend maturity date to reduce monthly payment, Reduce interest rate (rarely approved but possible), Temporarily reduce payment amount, Re-amortize based on current balance. PROS: Permanent change to loan terms, Can significantly reduce monthly payment, No new debt or lenders involved. CONS: Requires SBA approval (not guaranteed), May extend total repayment period, May increase total interest paid, Possible fees or conditions. BEST FOR: Permanent payment reduction needed, Business can't support current payment long-term. ALTERNATIVE 7: EQUITY FINANCING - Raise capital through equity instead of debt: Bring in new investor/partner, Sell portion of business ownership, Private equity or venture capital (if qualified), Crowdfunding equity raises. PROS: No debt repayment required, No subordination issues, Provides capital without monthly payments. CONS: Dilutes ownership, Loss of control to new owners, Complex legal and tax issues, Not available for all business types. BEST FOR: High-growth businesses attractive to investors, Situations where debt isn't appropriate, Owners willing to share ownership. ALTERNATIVE 8: ASSET SALE INSTEAD OF FINANCING - Sell non-critical assets to raise capital: Excess equipment or inventory, Real estate not essential to operations, Divisions or business units, Intellectual property or rights. PROS: Generates cash without debt, Simplifies business operations, No subordination needed, May have tax benefits. CONS: Permanently reduces business assets, May affect business capacity, One-time capital infusion only. BEST FOR: Businesses with non-essential assets, Simplification or downsizing scenarios. DECISION FRAMEWORK - WHICH ALTERNATIVE TO CHOOSE: Consider subordination if: 1) Need is significant ($100K+), 2) No other collateral available, 3) Comfortable with increased debt load, 4) Confident in repayment ability, 5) Willing to navigate complex process. Consider alternatives if: 1) Need is modest (<$100K), 2) Alternative sources of capital available, 3) Cash flow is tight, 4) Want to preserve collateral structure, 5) Seeking simpler, faster solution. BEST PRACTICE: Create comparison matrix: List all viable financing alternatives, Compare: Amount available, Cost (interest rate/fees), Time to obtain, Complexity, Risk level, Impact on business operations. Choose solution that best balances need, cost, risk, and complexity.

faqs.question: What documents do I need to submit for a COVID EIDL subordination?
faqs.answer: For a business asset (lien) subordination, you need: (1) Detailed borrower request letter explaining intended use of funds, (2) Signed SBA Form 2518 (Consent to Verify Information) for all borrowers/guarantors, (3) Copy of UCC Lien Search showing SBA's lien position, (4) Proof of Insurance as required by your Loan Authorization & Agreement, (5) Lender Loan Commitment Agreement or Term Sheet. For real estate subordinations, you ALSO need: (6) Settlement Statement(s), (7) Title Report dated within 6 months, (8) Current Valuation Report/Appraisal, (9) Payoff Statement(s) for existing liens, (10) Current signed Profit & Loss (if cash-out or expansion), (11) Copy of Mortgage, Deed of Trust, or Other Lien Instrument. See the CESC Subordination Requirements Letter at SBA.gov for complete details.

faqs.question: Where do I submit my COVID EIDL subordination packet?
faqs.answer: Submit your complete subordination package to the COVID EIDL Servicing Center using ONE method only: (1) EMAIL: COVIDEIDLServicing@sba.gov (use clear subject line with loan number), (2) SECURE UPLOAD: Box.com portal (request link from SBA via email first), or (3) FAX: (202) 481-5799. Do NOT submit through multiple channels as this causes processing delays. For questions only (not submissions), call (833) 853-5638, Monday-Friday 8am-8pm ET. Always include your SBA loan number in all communications.

faqs.question: Do I need a title report for real estate subordination?
faqs.answer: Yes, for real estate subordinations you MUST provide a current title report dated within the past 6 months. This document verifies property ownership, existing liens and their priority, any title defects or encumbrances, and the SBA's current lien position. The title report must clearly show Schedule A (property description/ownership) and Schedule B (all exceptions including liens in priority order). Order from your title company or underwriting agency. Title reports older than 6 months will be rejected as outdated. This is required in addition to all general subordination documents.

faqs.question: What is SBA Form 2518 and why is it required?
faqs.answer: SBA Form 2518 (Consent to Verify Information and 3rd Party Authorization) is an official SBA document that grants the SBA written permission to: (1) Verify your financial, credit, and collateral information with third parties, (2) Communicate with your new lender, title company, attorney, accountant, or other parties involved in the transaction, (3) Access financial records and credit reports as needed for subordination review. This form MUST be completed, signed, and dated by ALL borrowers AND guarantors listed on your original COVID EIDL. Without properly executed Form 2518, the SBA cannot process your subordination request or communicate with your lender. Download at: https://www.sba.gov/document/sba-form-borrowers-consent-verify-information-third-party-authorization

faqs.question: How do I write the subordination request letter?
faqs.answer: Your borrower request letter should be on company letterhead (if available) and include: (1) SBA loan number and business name, (2) WHY you need subordination - specific business situation requiring new financing, (3) HOW it benefits your business - lower rates, improved cash flow, working capital for growth, etc., (4) HOW funds will be used - specific details (equipment purchase, refinancing, inventory, expansion), (5) YOUR ability to repay BOTH loans - demonstrate new financing strengthens (not weakens) your ability to service COVID EIDL. Be professional, specific, and include property/asset description being subordinated. The SBA wants to see this makes good business sense and improves your position as a borrower, not just adds more debt. Must be signed by authorized representative.

faqs.question: How long does COVID EIDL subordination approval take?
faqs.answer: Typical timeline: Day 1-2: SBA confirms receipt, Day 3-7: Initial document completeness review, Day 7-21: Detailed underwriting and collateral analysis, Day 14-30: Final decision and notification. IMPORTANT: The SBA does not publish guaranteed timeframes due to variable volume. Complete, well-organized submissions process faster than incomplete packages. Real estate subordinations typically take longer than business asset subordinations. If additional information is requested, the timeline restarts. Processing may be faster during low-volume periods. BEST PRACTICE: Follow up 5-7 business days after submission to confirm receipt and ask for timeline estimate from your assigned specialist.

faqs.question: Can I apply for subordination if I'm behind on COVID EIDL payments?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can apply for subordination even if you're behind on payments, BUT approval is significantly less likely and will come with strict conditions. You must: (1) Disclose default status in your borrower letter - hiding it will result in automatic denial and potential fraud charges, (2) Be transparent about reasons for default and your cure plan. The SBA will typically require: (1) Payment of all past-due amounts to bring account current, (2) Demonstration of consistent payment history for 3-6 months, (3) Resolution of any non-monetary defaults (insurance, etc.). The SBA may condition approval on using new loan proceeds to cure the default. If in default due to hardship, consider Hardship Accommodation Plan (HAP) first before seeking subordination. Approval rates are lower for defaulted loans.

faqs.question: Is there a fee for COVID EIDL subordination?
faqs.answer: The SBA does not charge a fee for processing COVID EIDL subordination requests. However, YOU will incur costs: (1) Your new lender may charge origination fees, points, or other loan fees, (2) Title company charges for title reports, settlement services, recording fees (for real estate), (3) Appraisal fees ($2,000-$10,000+ depending on property type and complexity), (4) UCC search fees (typically $25-$100 per search), (5) Attorney fees if you hire legal counsel ($2,000-$10,000+ for complex matters), (6) Environmental assessment costs if required for real estate ($2,000-$50,000+ depending on scope). Recording fees for the final subordination documents vary by location and are typically paid at closing.

faqs.question: What if my COVID EIDL subordination request is denied?
faqs.answer: If denied, you have several options: OPTION 1 - Address Issues & Resubmit: Fix specific problems cited in denial letter, submit as NEW request (not reconsideration) after waiting at least 30 days, include cover letter explaining changes made, strengthen your financial position or collateral coverage. OPTION 2 - Request Reconsideration: Use if you believe SBA made an error in analysis, email COVIDEIDLServicing@sba.gov or use MySBA portal, provide specific explanation of error with supporting docs, submit within timeframe in denial letter (typically 30 days). OPTION 3 - Modify Approach: Request smaller loan amount, explore financing that doesn't require subordination, offer partial EIDL paydown, strengthen business position before reapplying. Common denial reasons: insufficient collateral, weak justification, incomplete docs, increased default risk, poor payment history.

faqs.question: Do I need hazard insurance for subordination?
faqs.answer: Yes, you must provide proof of current hazard insurance coverage as required by your SBA Loan Authorization & Agreement (LA&A). For COVID EIDL loans over $25,000 involving business property collateral, you need: (1) Hazard insurance protecting against fire, lightning, and extended perils, (2) Coverage equal to at least 80% of insurable collateral value, (3) Policy maintained throughout full loan term, (4) SBA listed as loss payee or mortgagee/additional insured. Request Certificate of Insurance from your provider showing: business name, property address/description, coverage limits, policy dates, SBA properly listed. If leasing, check whether landlord's policy covers you or if you need separate coverage. Lapsed or insufficient insurance can trigger default.

faqs.question: What is the SBA's lien position on my COVID EIDL?
faqs.answer: The SBA's lien position refers to their priority claim on your collateral. For COVID EIDL loans over $25,000, the SBA filed a UCC-1 Financing Statement creating a blanket security interest (lien) on ALL business assets including equipment, inventory, accounts receivable, and fixtures. For loans over $200,000, the SBA may also have placed a lien on real estate. The UCC-1 filing establishes priority - if assets are liquidated, the SBA has legal claim to proceeds based on their position (first, second, third, etc.). Subordination allows another lender to move ahead of the SBA's position, meaning in liquidation the new lender gets paid first before SBA receives anything. This is why SBA carefully evaluates whether sufficient collateral value remains after subordination.

faqs.question: Can I request subordination multiple times?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can request subordination multiple times on the same COVID EIDL loan IF sufficient equity remains in the collateral to protect the SBA's interest. Each request is evaluated independently based on current circumstances including: (1) Current collateral values, (2) Total debt secured by the collateral, (3) Your payment history on all loans, (4) Business financial condition, (5) Cumulative effect of all subordinations on SBA's security position. However, each additional subordination: (1) Increases your total debt load and monthly payments, (2) Further weakens SBA's collateral position, (3) Increases likelihood of denial, (4) May require stronger financial justification. Multiple subordinations are common for working capital lines that renew annually, but full refinancing subordinations are typically one-time events.

faqs.question: What is a UCC Lien Search and how do I get one?
faqs.answer: A UCC (Uniform Commercial Code) Lien Search is a search of public records showing all filed liens against your business assets. It reveals: (1) Who has claims on your business assets, (2) What assets are covered by each lien, (3) Filing dates (which establish priority), (4) Current status of liens. To obtain: (1) Visit your state's Secretary of State website or UCC filing portal (Example: Florida = Sunbiz.org), (2) Search using exact legal business name from loan docs, (3) Try name variations if needed (DBAs, former names), (4) Request within past 6 months of submission (older searches rejected), (5) Shows SBA's UCC-1 filing plus any other liens. Use National Association of Secretaries of State (NASS) directory for state portal links. The SBA uses this to evaluate lien priority and whether sufficient collateral value exists after subordination.

faqs.question: Does subordination release my personal guarantee on the COVID EIDL?
faqs.answer: NO - subordination does NOT release personal guarantees. Even after subordination is approved: (1) ALL personal guarantees remain in full effect, (2) Guarantors are still personally liable for the full COVID EIDL balance, (3) SBA can still pursue guarantors' personal assets (home, vehicles, accounts, investments) if business defaults, (4) Subordination only affects lien position on business/property collateral, NOT personal liability. In fact, subordination INCREASES risk to guarantors because: (1) Now there are TWO loans to repay, (2) Default on new loan may trigger cross-default on EIDL, (3) If business fails, SBA is in second position on business assets (new lender gets paid first), making SBA more likely to aggressively pursue personal guarantees. Personal guarantee release is a separate servicing action requiring different criteria.

faqs.question: What happens if I grant a lien to another lender without SBA subordination approval?
faqs.answer: Granting a lien to another creditor without SBA approval is a DEFAULT under your Loan Authorization & Agreement and can trigger serious consequences: (1) TECHNICAL DEFAULT - You've violated loan covenants even if making payments, (2) ACCELERATION - SBA can demand immediate payment of entire loan balance, (3) FORECLOSURE - SBA can begin collection actions including foreclosure on collateral, (4) PERSONAL GUARANTEE ENFORCEMENT - SBA can pursue your personal assets under guarantees, (5) NEW LOAN COMPLICATIONS - The unauthorized lien may be legally defective, preventing new lender from actually funding, (6) FUTURE REQUESTS - Damages relationship with SBA for future servicing requests. This is why you MUST get subordination approval BEFORE allowing new lender to file a superior lien. Never let new lender file UCC or mortgage before SBA approval is in hand.

faqs.question: Can I use subordination proceeds to pay down my COVID EIDL?
faqs.answer: It depends on the transaction structure and SBA approval conditions. GENERALLY: (1) For refinancing transactions, if you're consolidating other debt, you typically cannot use proceeds to pay down EIDL without SBA approval - the SBA must remain in their agreed position. (2) For cash-out refinancing, the SBA may REQUIRE partial paydown as a condition of approval if the transaction significantly weakens their collateral position. (3) For working capital lines, proceeds should be used for stated business purposes (inventory, receivables, operations), not to make EIDL payments. IMPORTANT: Using new debt to service old debt is generally viewed negatively - it suggests you can't afford both payments. The SBA wants to see the new financing STRENGTHENS your ability to repay EIDL from improved business operations, not that you're robbing Peter to pay Paul. Always disclose intended use of proceeds accurately in your borrower letter.

faqs.question: Do I need flood insurance for subordination?
faqs.answer: You need flood insurance IF: (1) Your improved real estate or personal property collateral is located in a FEMA-designated Special Flood Hazard Area (SFHA), AND (2) The community participates in the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP). Flood insurance is required for the LIFE of the loan and cannot be terminated or reduced without SBA approval. You must: (1) Obtain FEMA Form 81-93 (Standard Flood Hazard Determination Form) from your lender or flood determination company, (2) If property IS in SFHA, obtain flood insurance through NFIP or private carrier, (3) Maintain coverage at required levels throughout loan term, (4) Provide proof of coverage with subordination package. If insurance is not in place, SBA may force-place coverage at your expense. Flood insurance requirements are verified at subordination review - lapsed coverage can delay or prevent approval.

faqs.question: What is a Settlement Statement and why is it required?
faqs.answer: A Settlement Statement (also called HUD-1, ALTA Settlement Statement, or Closing Disclosure) is the final accounting document from a real estate closing showing: (1) Loan amount and all disbursements, (2) Payoffs to existing lienholders with amounts, (3) Title insurance and escrow charges, (4) Recording fees and other closing costs, (5) Net proceeds to borrower (if any), (6) Final distribution of all funds. Required ONLY for real estate subordinations, not business asset subordinations. The SBA uses it to verify: (1) Funds are being distributed as described in your request, (2) SBA's lien position is not being improperly paid off, (3) Transaction structure matches what was approved, (4) No unauthorized disbursements to borrower or third parties. Must be FINAL (executed with all signatures), not draft or estimate. Discrepancies between Settlement Statement and other docs (like payoff statements) will delay review.

faqs.question: How current does my Profit & Loss statement need to be?
faqs.answer: For real estate subordinations involving cash-out or business expansion, your Profit & Loss statement must be: (1) CURRENT - covering at least the most recent 6-12 months of operations, ideally year-to-date plus prior full year, (2) SIGNED AND DATED - by an authorized representative (owner, officer, or authorized signer), (3) RECENT - prepared within past 90 days of submission, (4) ACCURATE - matching your accounting records and reconcilable to tax returns, (5) COMPLETE - showing detailed breakdown of revenues, cost of goods sold, operating expenses, and net profit/loss. Can be prepared using accounting software (QuickBooks, Xero) or by your CPA. Should match format used in normal business reporting. The SBA reviews for: revenue trends, expense management, profitability, seasonal patterns, and overall financial stability. If showing losses, your justification for subordination must strongly explain how new financing improves financial performance.

faqs.question: What if I can't find all my original COVID EIDL documents?
faqs.answer: If you've lost your original COVID EIDL loan documents, you can obtain copies: (1) COVID EIDL DOCUMENTS - Email COVIDEIDLServicing@sba.gov requesting copies, include your loan number and specify which documents needed (Loan Authorization & Agreement, promissory note, security agreements, etc.), allow 5-10 business days for response. (2) UCC FILINGS - Request from your state's Secretary of State office, search their online UCC database for your business name, download/purchase certified copies if needed. (3) RECORDED REAL ESTATE DOCS - Request from county recorder's office where property is located, provide property address or legal description, may charge small fee for copies. (4) MySBA LOAN PORTAL - Login at lending.sba.gov, some documents may be available for download, view loan details and payment history. Create organized files immediately upon receiving copies to prevent future issues.

faqs.question: Can I subordinate part of my collateral but not all?
faqs.answer: Yes, PARTIAL SUBORDINATION is often possible and sometimes preferred by the SBA. Instead of subordinating the SBA's lien on ALL business assets, you can request subordination on specific assets only. EXAMPLES: (1) Subordinate lien on inventory and receivables for working capital line, while SBA retains first position on equipment, vehicles, and real estate. (2) Subordinate lien on specific equipment being financed, while SBA retains first position on all other assets. (3) Subordinate lien on one property, while retaining first position on other properties. ADVANTAGES: (1) Reduces risk to SBA (easier approval), (2) Preserves SBA's first position on most valuable/stable collateral, (3) Gives you flexibility for future financing, (4) Simpler documentation and analysis. REQUIREMENTS: (1) Must clearly identify specific assets being subordinated, (2) Must demonstrate adequate remaining collateral secures EIDL, (3) Lender commitment must specify exactly which assets they require first lien on. Partial subordination is common for equipment financing and working capital lines.

faqs.question: What is the difference between subordination and lien release?
faqs.answer: SUBORDINATION = SBA moves their lien to a lower priority position BUT the lien remains in place. You still owe the full EIDL amount. The new lender gets first position; SBA moves to second position. Both liens remain on the collateral. LIEN RELEASE = SBA completely removes their lien from specific collateral. That collateral is no longer pledged to secure the EIDL. Generally requires payment to SBA approximately equal to the value being released. WHEN EACH IS USED: Subordination - When you need new financing and the lender requires first lien, when refinancing existing debt, when total debt will remain roughly the same or increase. Lien Release - When selling collateral and SBA needs to release their claim, when paying down EIDL significantly and releasing excess collateral, when substituting different collateral. IMPORTANT: Subordination is NOT the same as loan forgiveness - you still owe the full EIDL balance and must make all payments.

faqs.question: Does my new lender need to wait for SBA approval before filing their lien?
faqs.answer: YES - absolutely critical. Your new lender MUST WAIT for written SBA subordination approval before filing their UCC-1 or mortgage. Here's why: (1) SEQUENCING MATTERS - The SBA subordination agreement must be in place BEFORE the new lien is filed, otherwise the new lien may be legally defective or junior to SBA. (2) DEFAULT RISK - If new lender files before SBA approval, you're in technical default on your EIDL for granting unauthorized superior lien. (3) CLOSING COMPLICATIONS - Premature filing can prevent closing or require unwinding and refiling. PROPER SEQUENCE: (1) Submit complete subordination request to SBA, (2) Wait for SBA written approval and subordination agreement, (3) Execute SBA subordination agreement, (4) THEN new lender can file their lien, (5) Record subordination agreement and new lien simultaneously or in proper order, (6) Provide recorded copies to SBA. Work closely with new lender and title company to ensure proper sequencing.

faqs.question: What is a Loan Authorization & Agreement (LA&A) and why does it matter?
faqs.answer: Your SBA Loan Authorization & Agreement (LA&A) is the master legal contract governing your COVID EIDL. It's your rulebook for the loan and contains: (1) LOAN TERMS - amount, interest rate, payment schedule, maturity date, (2) COLLATERAL REQUIREMENTS - what assets are pledged and insurance requirements, (3) USE OF PROCEEDS - what you can/cannot use loan funds for, (4) AFFIRMATIVE COVENANTS - things you MUST do (maintain insurance, pay taxes, provide financials, notify SBA of changes), (5) NEGATIVE COVENANTS - things you CANNOT do without SBA approval (sell business, change ownership, grant superior liens, liquidate assets), (6) DEFAULT PROVISIONS - what triggers default and consequences. WHY IT MATTERS FOR SUBORDINATION: Your LA&A prohibits granting superior liens without SBA approval - violating this is a default. It also specifies insurance and collateral requirements you must maintain. Before requesting subordination, read your LA&A to understand: current obligations, what changes require SBA approval, collateral already pledged, insurance requirements. Request copy from COVIDEIDLServicing@sba.gov if needed.

faqs.question: Can I request subordination before my COVID EIDL payments start?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can request subordination during the automatic 30-month deferment period (before payments begin). In fact, this may be strategically advantageous because: (1) Your payment history can't be evaluated (no negative payment history yet), (2) You may have stronger business financials if early in recovery, (3) Original collateral values may still be current. HOWEVER: (1) You still must meet all documentation requirements, (2) SBA will evaluate your ability to service BOTH loans once EIDL payments start, (3) Must demonstrate business generates sufficient cash flow to support both debts, (4) Any new debt affects your debt-to-income ratio when EIDL payments begin. TIMING CONSIDERATION: If requesting subordination during deferment, clearly explain in borrower letter: (1) When EIDL payments will start, (2) How business cash flow will support both loans, (3) Provide financial projections showing debt service coverage. The SBA will underwrite based on your ability to pay both loans simultaneously, not based on current state during deferment.

faqs.question: What happens to my COVID EIDL if I sell my business?
faqs.answer: If you sell your business, the COVID EIDL does NOT automatically disappear. Several scenarios: SCENARIO 1 - ASSUMPTION: Buyer assumes the COVID EIDL debt, SBA must approve assumption in advance (separate servicing request), Requires buyer qualification (credit, experience, financials), You may be released from personal guarantee (rare) or remain liable as backup, EIDL continues with new borrower. SCENARIO 2 - PAYOFF AT CLOSING: Sale proceeds used to pay off EIDL in full, SBA must provide payoff statement, Lien is released after payment, You're fully released from liability. SCENARIO 3 - SELLER REMAINS LIABLE: Business sold but EIDL remains in your name, You remain personally liable under guarantee, Must continue making payments even though you no longer own business, Very risky - avoid this scenario. SUBORDINATION IMPACT: If you have subordination and are selling: Must notify both SBA and subordinated lender, May need consent from both lenders, Subordination agreement may prohibit sale without consent, Much more complex transaction. BEST PRACTICE: Consult attorney before selling business with existing EIDL and subordination.

faqs.question: How does subordination affect my business credit?
faqs.answer: Subordination itself typically does NOT directly affect your business credit score, but related factors do: POSITIVE IMPACTS: (1) If new loan has better terms and you make payments on time, may improve credit over time, (2) Increased available credit may improve credit utilization ratios, (3) Diversified credit mix (multiple lenders) can be positive. NEGATIVE IMPACTS: (1) Hard inquiry from new lender may temporarily lower score, (2) Increased total debt may affect debt-to-income ratios, (3) New account lowers average age of accounts, (4) If you struggle to make payments on both loans and become delinquent, significant negative impact. CREDIT REPORTING: (1) COVID EIDL is reported to business credit bureaus (Dun & Bradstreet, Experian Business, Equifax Business), (2) New subordinated loan will also be reported, (3) Payment history on both loans affects credit, (4) Subordination agreement itself is not directly reported. BEST PRACTICE: Before subordination, pull business credit reports to understand current status. After subordination, monitor both personal and business credit to ensure both loans are reporting correctly and no errors appear.

faqs.question: What are cross-default clauses and why should I care?
faqs.answer: A CROSS-DEFAULT CLAUSE means if you default on one loan, you're automatically in default on another loan, even if making payments on the second loan. Example: You have COVID EIDL (Loan A) and new equipment loan (Loan B), both with cross-default clauses. If you default on EIDL → automatic default on equipment loan. If you default on equipment loan → automatic default on EIDL. WHY THIS MATTERS FOR SUBORDINATION: (1) You're adding new debt with potential cross-default provisions, (2) Missing one payment on either loan can trigger: Acceleration of both loans (full balances due immediately), Multiple foreclosure actions simultaneously, Enforcement of personal guarantees on both loans, Cascading defaults on any other cross-defaulted debt, Severe damage to business and personal credit. WHAT TO DO: (1) Review your EIDL LA&A for cross-default provisions, (2) Review new lender's loan agreement carefully before signing, (3) Understand all triggers: missed payments, covenant violations, insurance lapses, tax liens, bankruptcy, judgments, (4) Maintain adequate cash reserves to prevent missed payments, (5) Set up automatic payments on both loans, (6) Communicate with both lenders immediately if problems arise. Cross-default dramatically increases risk of subordination.
